ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for kee chang / alkaline glutinous rice dumpling; mapped to closest parent class 'glutinous rice food product'.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for kee chang; A00LC (rice-based dish) used as closest defensible anchor. Alkaline treatment and leaf-wrapping are not captured in the code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for kee chang or alkaline glutinous rice dumpling.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical kee chang piece weighs approximately 80–120 g; a standard serving reference of 100 g is used here.

nutrient_source.title: Kee Chang (Glutinous Rice Dumpling, Alkaline)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Kee Chang (cr_id F05100062). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kee chang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 86 is assigned based on the closest analogous food: glutinous rice (white, steamed), GI = 86 (±10), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Alkaline treatment (lye water) does not substantially alter the starch gelatinisation profile of glutinous rice; the GI is therefore treated as equivalent. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (86 × 38.95) / 100 = 33.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 172 mg/100 g combined with high glycaemic load and negligible potassium
hypertension.guidance: Kee chang contains 172 mg sodium per 100 g and virtually no potassium, producing an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small piece and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces or sugar syrups that further worsen the cardiometabolic profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic index (GI 86) from glutinous rice base with negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kee chang is made from glutinous rice with a GI of approximately 86 and only 0.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, resulting in a glycaemic load of ~33.5 per 100 g serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption and, if eaten, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High refined-carbohydrate load with low fibre and low micronutrient density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kee chang is a high-GI, low-fibre refined carbohydrate food that contributes to postprandial triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance — both cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat it as an occasional food, limit to one small piece, and avoid sugar syrup accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-GI glutinous rice base with no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, no polyphenols, fibre 0.3 g/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kee chang provides no anti-inflammatory factors and its high glycaemic load promotes pro-inflammatory mediator production; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid it, particularly during flares, and replace with lower-GI whole-grain or legume-based alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kee chang (碱水粽 / 鹼水粽; also known as alkaline rice dumpling or lye water dumpling) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese and Peranakan food made from glutinous rice that has been soaked in lye water (alkaline solution), wrapped tightly in dried bamboo leaves, and steamed until fully cooked. The alkaline treatment gives the rice a distinctive pale yellow or golden colour, a slightly translucent appearance, a firm yet chewy and sticky texture, and a mild, subtly alkaline flavour with a faint grassy note from the bamboo leaves. Kee chang is typically served plain or with sweet accompaniments such as sugar syrup, kaya (coconut egg jam), or palm sugar, making it a popular breakfast and festive snack item in Singapore, Malaysia, and among the Hokkien and Teochew diaspora communities. Nutritionally, kee chang is a high-carbohydrate, low-fat, low-protein food: per 100 g it provides 171 kcal, 38.95 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g protein, 0.4 g fat, 7.66 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 172 mg sodium. The glycaemic index of glutinous rice is approximately 86 (high), producing a glycaemic load of ~33.5 per 100 g, making kee chang unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It contains no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients and is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Calcium is 6 mg/100 g and iron is 0.4 mg/100 g. The food is naturally dairy-free and vegan in its plain form, though accompaniments may alter this status.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of kee chang?
How many calories in kee chang?
Is kee chang the same as zong zi?
What does kee chang taste like?
Can hypertensive patients eat kee chang?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F05100062) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for glutinous rice (GI 86), which is the primary ingredient. Saturated fat estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for plain glutinous rice (negligible saturated fat in a low-fat food). All other fields are well-supported.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for kee chang (alkaline glutinous rice dumpling) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F05100062, and no equivalent complete panel was found in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 0.1 g/100 g from USDA SR Legacy (glutinous rice, cooked) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is a trace value consistent with the very low total fat of 0.4 g/100 g. 3. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate for glutinous rice (steamed) from Atkinson et al. 2008 / University of Sydney GI Database; no direct kee chang GI measurement is available in approved sources. 4. Halal status is marked false as kee chang may be prepared with lye water of varying composition; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 5. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (2.8), fat_g (0.4), and carbohydrate_g (38.95) are all non-zero.
